en.extract:
The Hispaniolan parrot (Amazona ventralis), also known as Hispaniolan amazon, is a species of psittaciform bird of the family Psittacidae endemic to Hispaniola, which includes the Dominican Republic and Haiti. Its white forehead, light beak, white eye-ring, blue ear patch, and maroon belly patch are the main features that differentiate it from other amazons. Its body measures about 28 centimeters in length. A typical adult weighs 250g (8.75 oz).
This species was widespread in Hispaniola, but it suffered a significant decline during the twentieth century. Its range was mainly limited to inland mountains in the 1930s and is becoming more typical in metropolitan cities such as Santo Domingo.


Taxonomy
German biologist Philipp Ludwig Statius Müller published the first scientific description in 1776. In 1535, Spanish historian Gonzalo Fernández de Oviedo published written descriptions of the Hispaniolan parrot. Oviedo describes them as Higuacas name derived from the Taíno word for these birds. Oviedo describes them:

In this island are three species of them large some, others minor and very small. The great ones are called by the Indians Higuacas, the middle long syllable [stressed], and these differ from those of the other islands in that they have a white forehead, not green or red. Those of this type in Cuba have a red forehead-heads. These are very talkative when taught to speak human words. The other
the type, medium-sized, is called xaxabis. They are much greener and some have red featherers. They are very noisy, nervous and restless. They bite and are more irritable than the others. They never learn anything about the human speak no matter how much they are taught, but they are very talkative in his own language. Ten of these xaxabis attack a hundred higuacas and take them down and they never meet together in peace with each other. Each species comes in a flock to get together a lot and where one goes everyone goes, each species calls in its own way. The figs have a louder sound and deeper, the xaxabis sharper and higher launched. Although the Xaxabis do not speak human's words, still, placed in a cage is a pleasure to see them.
Amazona ventralis, Amazona leucocephala, and Amazona vittata are all believed to be descendants of Amazona albifrons a Central American parrot. Based on mitochondrial DNA, the colonization of Hispaniola by the Amazon parrots occurred circa 760,000 years ago.


Description
They have bright green feathers with blue edges on most of them. They feature black ear coverts, some blue patches on the cheeks and crown, some red patches under the chin, and a noticeable white forehead and area surrounding the eyes. Additionally, they have red feathers on their abdomens, blue wind coverts, green outer web edges, yellowish green underfur, a green tail with yellow tips on the upper side, red outer tail feathers at the base, a horn-colored bill, and brown eyes with pale feet. Their bodies measure approximately 28 centimeters long. A typical adult weights 250g (8.75 oz), and they have a large, robust beak.


Ecology
They inhabit a variety of wooded environments around 1,500 meters above sea level, from arid savanna palm groves, pine forests, and humid mountain forests. They frequently forage on cultivated lands, such as banana plantations and cornfields. They are found at all elevations in forests, groves, and thickets, wherever there are suitable fruits and seeds. Populations have been limited to forest reserves and national parks such as Jaragua, Cotubanamá, and Los Haitises. They consume seeds or fruits of Caesalpinia, Psidium and Ficus.


Population
The population is estimated to be around 10,000-19,999 individuals. This equates to 6,667-13,333 mature individuals, rounded here to 6,000-15,000 mature individuals.


Breeding
Parrots form a couple for life, and lay 2–4 eggs per clutch. They typically nest in cavities in trees up to 20 meters above the ground, but nests have been found as low as 1.5 meters. It has also been reported nesting on rocky ledges. Breeding between the months of February and June. Prospecting pairs have been observed in mid-April, suggesting that the season may extend further in the year.


Threats
Traffickers removing chicks from their nests can destroy the cavities in the trees that the parrots reuse each season, reducing the likelihood of parrots being able to successfully nest. Additionally, they are hunted for food, trapped for local trade, and are trapped for international trade as cage birds. It is also treated as a crop pest.
The trade or possession of parrots is banned under Dominican Republic law.


References


External links

Species Factsheet Archived 23 November 2008 at the Wayback Machine, BirdLife
Hispaniolan Amazon videos, images and sounds, Internet Bird Collection]

http://www.grupojaragua.org.do/nuestro-trabajo/defensoria/nomedecotorra/ Archived 28 January 2023 at the Wayback Machine

The '''Hispaniolan parrot''' ('''''Amazona ventralis'''''), also known as '''Hispaniolan amazon''', is a species of [[Parrot|psittaciform]] bird of the family [[Psittacidae]] endemic to [[Hispaniola]], which includes the [[Dominican Republic]] and [[Haiti]]. Its white forehead, light beak, white eye-ring, blue ear patch, and maroon belly patch are the main features that differentiate it from other amazons. Its body measures about 28 centimeters in length. A typical adult weighs 250g (8.75 oz).<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hispaniolan Amazon (Amazona ventralis) {{!}} Parrot Encyclopedia |url=https://www.parrots.org/encyclopedia/hispaniolan-amazon |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=www.parrots.org}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Hispaniolan Amazon (Amazona ventralis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/hispaniolan-amazon-amazona-ventralis |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref>

This species was widespread in Hispaniola, but it suffered a significant decline during the twentieth century. Its range was mainly limited to inland mountains in the 1930s and is becoming more typical in metropolitan cities such as [[Santo Domingo]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hispaniolan Amazon Long-term survival of the Hispaniolan Amazon and the Trogon Parakeet {{!}} Loro Parque Fundación |url=https://www.loroparque-fundacion.org/en/portfolio/amazona-ventralis-dominican-republic-hispaniolan-amazon |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=www.loroparque-fundacion.org}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
German biologist [[Philipp Ludwig Statius Müller]] published the first scientific description in 1776.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Amazona ventralis (Statius Muller, 1776) |url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=177803#null |access-date=2023-04-29}}</ref> In 1535, Spanish historian [[Gonzalo Fernández de Oviedo y Valdés|Gonzalo Fernández de Oviedo]] published written descriptions of the Hispaniolan parrot.<ref>{{Cite web |title=About the Natural History of the Indies. |url=https://www.loc.gov/item/2021666764/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Library of Congress, Washington, D.C. 20540 USA}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Alas & Colores {{!}} Aves endémicas de La Española - Cotorra de La Española |url=http://alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es/es/aves/cotorra-de-la-espanola |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es |archive-date=3 May 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230503065235/http://alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es/es/aves/cotorra-de-la-espanola |url-status=dead }}</ref> Oviedo describes them as Higuacas<ref>{{Cite web |last=ASALE |title=higuaca {{!}} Diccionario de americanismos |url=https://www.asale.org/damer/higuaca |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=«Diccionario de americanismos» |language=es}}</ref> name derived from the Taíno word for these birds.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Alas & Colores {{!}} Aves endémicas de La Española - Cotorra de La Española |url=http://alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es/es/aves/cotorra-de-la-espanola |access-date=2023-04-30 |website=alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es |archive-date=3 May 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230503065235/http://alasycolores.embajadadominicana.es/es/aves/cotorra-de-la-espanola |url-status=dead }}</ref> Oviedo describes them:
{{Blockquote|text=In this island are three species of them large some, others minor and very small. The great ones are called by the Indians Higuacas, the middle long syllable [stressed], and these differ from those of the other islands in that they have a white forehead, not green or red. Those of this type in Cuba have a red forehead-heads. These are very talkative when taught to speak human words. The other
the type, medium-sized, is called xaxabis. They are much greener and some have red featherers. They are very noisy, nervous and restless. They bite and are more irritable than the others. They never learn anything about the human speak no matter how much they are taught, but they are very talkative in his own language. Ten of these xaxabis attack a hundred higuacas and take them down and they never meet together in peace with each other. Each species comes in a flock to get together a lot and where one goes everyone goes, each species calls in its own way. The figs have a louder sound and deeper, the xaxabis sharper and higher launched. Although the Xaxabis do not speak human's words, still, placed in a cage is a pleasure to see them.|author=Gonzalo Fernández de Oviedo|title=De la natural hystoria de las Indias}}
''Amazona ventralis'', [[Cuban amazon|''Amazona leucocephala'']], and [[Puerto Rican amazon|''Amazona vittata'']] are all believed to be descendants of [[White-fronted amazon|''Amazona albifrons'']] a Central American parrot.<ref>{{Cite journal |title=Molecular Phylogeny and Evolution of Amazon Parrots in the Greater Antilles |date=2021 |pmc=8074781 |last1=Kolchanova |first1=S. |last2=Komissarov |first2=A. |last3=Kliver |first3=S. |last4=Mazo-Vargas |first4=A. |last5=Afanador |first5=Y. |last6=Velez-Valentín |first6=J. |last7=de la Rosa |first7=R. V. |last8=Castro-Marquez |first8=S. |last9=Rivera-Colon |first9=I. |last10=Majeske |first10=A. J. |last11=Wolfsberger |first11=W. W. |last12=Hains |first12=T. |last13=Corvelo |first13=A. |last14=Martinez-Cruzado |first14=J. C. |last15=Glenn |first15=T. C. |last16=Robinson |first16=O. |last17=Koepfli |first17=K. P. |last18=Oleksyk |first18=T. K. |journal=Genes |volume=12 |issue=4 |page=608 |doi=10.3390/genes12040608 |pmid=33924228 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Based on mitochondrial DNA, the colonization of Hispaniola by the Amazon parrots occurred circa 760,000 years ago.<ref>{{Cite web |last=jenteottie |date=2022-07-16 |title=How Amazon parrots spread across the Greater Antilles |url=https://avianhybrids.wordpress.com/2022/07/16/how-amazon-parrots-spread-across-the-greater-antilles/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Avian Hybrids |language=en}}</ref>

== Description ==
They have bright green feathers with blue edges on most of them. They feature black ear coverts, some blue patches on the cheeks and crown, some red patches under the chin, and a noticeable white forehead and area surrounding the eyes. Additionally, they have red feathers on their abdomens, blue wind coverts, green outer web edges, yellowish green underfur, a green tail with yellow tips on the upper side, red outer tail feathers at the base, a horn-colored bill, and brown eyes with pale feet. Their bodies measure approximately 28 centimeters long. A typical adult weights 250g (8.75 oz), and they have a large, robust beak.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hispaniolan Amazon Facts and Information {{!}} SeaWorld Parks & Entertainment |url=https://seaworld.org/animals/facts/birds/hispaniolan-amazon/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=seaworld.org |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |first=Joseph M. |last=Forshaw |author-link=Joseph Forshaw |title=Parrots of the World; an Identification Guide|url=https://archive.org/details/parrotsofworldid0000fors |url-access=registration |others =Illustrated by Frank Knight|publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |isbn=0-691-09251-6 |year=2006 }}</ref>

=== Ecology ===
They inhabit a variety of wooded environments around 1,500 meters above sea level, from arid savanna palm groves, pine forests, and humid mountain forests. They frequently forage on cultivated lands, such as banana plantations and cornfields. They are found at all elevations in forests, groves, and thickets, wherever there are suitable fruits and seeds. Populations have been limited to forest reserves and national parks such as [[Jaragua National Park|Jaragua]], [[Cotubanamá National Park|Cotubanamá]], and [[Los Haitises National Park|Los Haitises]].<ref name="No me de cotorra">{{Cite web |title=No me de cotorra |url=http://www.grupojaragua.org.do/nuestro-trabajo/defensoria/nomedecotorra/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |language=es-ES |archive-date=28 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230128220136/http://www.grupojaragua.org.do/nuestro-trabajo/defensoria/nomedecotorra/ |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{Cite report |title=Monitoreo y Construcción de Nidos para la Protección de la Cotorra de La Hispaniola (''Amazona ventralis'') en el Parque Nacional Jaragua, República Dominicana. Informe Final de Proyecto. |publication-date=2009-08-26 |last1=León |first1=Yolanda M. |last2=Garrido |first2=Esteban |publisher=Grupo Jaragua, Santo Domingo |url=https://bvearmb.do/handle/123456789/971 |lang=es}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Collar |first1=Nigel |last2=Boesman |first2=Peter F. D. |last3=Sharpe |first3=Christopher J. |date=2020 |title=Hispaniolan Parrot (Amazona ventralis), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/hispar1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.hispar1.01|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They consume seeds or fruits of ''[[Caesalpinia]]'', ''[[Psidium]]'' and ''[[Ficus]].''<ref name="Pets">{{Cite web |last=Pets |title=Hispaniolan Parrot (Amazona ventralis) - Exotic birds |url=https://www.mascotarios.org/amazona-la-espanola/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Pets |language=en}}</ref>

=== Population ===
The population is estimated to be around 10,000-19,999 individuals. This equates to 6,667-13,333 mature individuals, rounded here to 6,000-15,000 mature individuals.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hispaniolan Amazon (Amazona ventralis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/hispaniolan-amazon-amazona-ventralis/text |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
Parrots form a couple for life,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Cotorra de la Española |url=https://zoodom.gob.do/cotorra-de-la-espanola/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Parque Zoológico Nacional {{!}} ZOODOM |language=es}}</ref> and lay 2–4 eggs per clutch. They typically nest in cavities in trees up to 20 meters above the ground, but nests have been found as low as 1.5 meters. It has also been reported nesting on rocky ledges. Breeding between the months of February and June. Prospecting pairs have been observed in mid-April, suggesting that the season may extend further in the year.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dominguez |first=Henry Abreu |date=2022-02-01 |title=Cotorra |url=https://faunadominicana.com/cotorra |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Fauna Dominicana |language=es-MX}}</ref>

== Threats ==

Traffickers removing chicks from their nests can destroy the cavities in the trees that the parrots reuse each season, reducing the likelihood of parrots being able to successfully nest.<ref name="No me de cotorra"/> Additionally, they are hunted for food, trapped for local trade, and are trapped for [[International parrot trade|international trade]] as cage birds. It is also treated as a crop pest.<ref name="Pets"/>

The trade or possession of parrots is banned under Dominican Republic law.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-06-21 |title=Medio Ambiente incauta ejemplares de cotorras en peligro de extinción que iban a ser comercializadas de manera ilegal |url=https://hoy.com.do/medio-ambiente-incauta-ejemplares-de-cotorras-en-peligro-de-extincion-que-iban-a-ser-comercializadas-de-manera-ilegal/ |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=Hoy Digital |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Grupo Jaragua - Deja eso |url=http://www.grupojaragua.org.do/dejaeso_english.html |access-date=2023-04-29 |website=www.grupojaragua.org.do}}</ref>
